In a bad offense with a rigid coordinator, he's a returner who occasionally catches a pass. At pick 2.24, you're paying for the upside and accepting the floor.
concepcion.verdict: Useful slot/returner but not a dynasty building block. Draft for depth.

sadiq.shortReport: The prize of a weak tight end class. Sadiq is a 6'6" 241-pound former basketball player whose athletic profile is rare for the position — he moves with the fluidity of a player 30 pounds lighter, wins above the rim in the red zone with a 34.1-inch vertical, and shows the kind of ascending production (2→24→36 catches, 0→2→5 TDs) that suggests he's just beginning to unlock his potential. He's raw as a blocker and his route-running is still developing, but the athletic foundation is so strong that he projects as a move TE who can create mismatches in the slot from Day 1. In a dynasty landscape where tight end is the scarcest position, Sadiq's combination of size, athleticism, and trajectory makes him the most valuable TE in this class.
sadiq.athleticProfile: Sadiq's movement profile is defined by the kind of fluidity that you almost never see at 6'6" 241 pounds. He can sink his hips and break like a receiver 30 pounds lighter, which allows him to run route concepts that most tight ends simply can't execute. His basketball background shows up in his body control — he can adjust to balls thrown behind him, above him, or away from his frame without losing his balance or his momentum. The 34.1-inch vertical is a measurable confirmation of the explosiveness you see on tape: he wins jump balls against defenders who are in position but can't match his leap. He's a long-strider who covers ground quickly in the open field, which gives him after-the-catch ability that most tight ends lack — he's not just a big target who catches and falls down. He stiff-arms smaller defenders and hurdles diving tacklers in space. The raw element of his profile is the blocking — he doesn't have the technique or the leverage to hold up as an inline blocker, which means he'll be a move TE or F-back rather than a traditional three-down tight end. But that's fine for fantasy purposes — his value is as a receiver, not a blocker.
strengths: The athletic profile is the foundation and it's rare for the position. At 6'6" 241 with a basketball background and a 34.1-inch vertical, Sadiq has the kind of size-athleticism combination that creates mismatches wherever he lines up. Put him inline and he's too big for linebackers. Put him in the slot and he's too fast for safeties. Put him on the boundary in goal-line packages and he's too tall for corners. That mismatch potential is what makes him the most valuable tight end in this class — NFL offensive coordinators will design plays specifically to exploit the physical advantages he creates.

The red-zone production is the most immediately translatable skill. Five touchdowns on 36 catches in 2025 isn't dominant volume, but it's an elite touchdown rate that reflects his ability to win above the rim. In the red zone, where the field compresses and windows tighten, a tight end who can elevate and catch the ball at its highest point is a quarterback's best friend. Sadiq does that consistently — he times his jump, extends his long arms, and catches the ball at a point that defenders simply can't reach. That skill translates immediately to the NFL because it doesn't depend on route-running polish or blocking technique.

The route-running trajectory is steep and encouraging. In 2023, he ran two routes and caught two balls — he was essentially an athlete playing tight end. By 2025, he's running seam routes, corner routes, curl-stop concepts, and even some option routes from the slot. The breaks aren't as sharp as a receiver's — he still rounds off some stems at the top — but the improvement from year to year is dramatic. If he continues on this trajectory, he could be running a complete route tree within two NFL seasons. That's the kind of development curve that makes a tight end a dynasty asset.

The after-the-catch ability separates him from the big-target tight ends who catch and collapse. Sadiq doesn't just catch the ball and wait for contact — he catches, turns upfield, and creates with the kind of stiff-arms and hurdles that you'd expect from a player with his basketball background. He understands leverage and body positioning in space, which allows him to slip tackles that would bring down most tight ends. That YAC ability adds a dimension of fantasy value that pure catch-and-fall tight ends lack.
weaknesses: The blocking is a significant technical deficit that will limit his inline snaps and therefore his overall playing time. He doesn't have the hand placement, the leverage, or the understanding of blocking angles that NFL tight ends need to hold up against defensive ends and outside linebackers. He'll be a move TE or F-back who lines up in the slot, in the backfield, or in a wing position — not a traditional inline tight end who can stay on the field for every play. That limits his snap count and therefore his target volume, which caps his fantasy floor.

The production is still modest by elite tight end standards. Thirty-six catches and five touchdowns in a season is a solid TE2 line, but it's not the kind of volume that projects to a weekly TE1 in fantasy. The ascending trajectory (2→24→36) suggests the volume is still growing, but we're projecting future production rather than confirming it. There's a risk that 36 catches is closer to his ceiling than his floor — that he's a complementary piece rather than a focal point of the passing game.

He can drift on routes when the play breaks down, which is a habit that stems from his basketball instincts — he's used to moving without the ball and finding open space, which in football means he sometimes abandons his route before the quarterback expects him to. That creates timing issues and potential turnovers. It's a correctable habit, but it's one that will frustrate quarterbacks and coordinators in his early NFL seasons.

The one-year breakout concern applies here too. His 2023 season (2 catches) was essentially invisible. He's had one semi-productive season (2024: 24 catches) and one solid season (2025: 36 catches). That's not enough of a track record to project with confidence. He could be an ascending talent who continues to improve, or he could be a late bloomer who's already close to his ceiling.
sadiq.nflFit:
Sadiq is a move TE in a spread offense who creates mismatches as an F-back or jumbo slot. He won't be an every-down inline tight end — his value is as a receiving weapon who can be moved around the formation to exploit defensive weaknesses. The ideal fit is a team with a creative offensive coordinator who uses 12-personnel (one back, two tight ends) and features the tight end in the passing game. Think Kansas City, Detroit, or San Francisco — all teams that have made tight ends productive fantasy assets by designing their offenses around the position's mismatch potential.

The mismatch creation is the core of his value. At 6'6" 241 with a 34.1-inch vertical, Sadiq is too big for linebackers and too fast for safeties. That mismatch exists regardless of scheme — it's a product of his physical profile. But a creative coordinator will design plays specifically to exploit it: red-zone fades, seam routes against single-high, slot option routes against zone. A rigid coordinator will just line him up inline and run him on basic routes, which wastes the mismatch potential.

The blocking limitation means he'll never be a three-down inline tight end, which caps his snap count at roughly 70% of offensive plays. That's enough for fantasy relevance if the targets are there, but it's not enough for the kind of every-down usage that elite tight ends get. His fantasy ceiling depends on whether a coordinator can design a role that maximizes his receiving ability without requiring him to block.
sadiq.comp:
The ceiling comparison is Evan Engram — a move TE whose athletic profile creates mismatches in the slot and whose receiving production scales with usage. Sadiq isn't as polished a route runner as Engram was coming out, but the athletic profile is similar. The floor comparison is Cole Kmet — a big tight end who produces modestly as a receiver but doesn't have the athletic upside to become a true difference-maker.

The Engram comparison is about the mismatch profile specifically. Engram has been one of the most productive fantasy tight ends because his athletic profile creates favorable matchups that coordinators can exploit. Sadiq has a similar mismatch profile — too big for linebackers, too fast for safeties — which means his production will scale with usage in the same way. If he gets 100 targets, he'll produce a TE1 season. If he gets 50 targets, he'll produce a TE2 season.

The Kmet comparison is the floor if the route-running doesn't develop. Kmet is a big, reliable target who produces 50-60 catches per season but doesn't have the athletic upside to become a true difference-maker. If Sadiq's route tree stalls at its current level, he becomes a bigger version of Kmet — productive but not elite.
sadiq.dynastyOutlook:
Sadiq is the TE1 in this class with weekly TE1 upside. In a weak tight end class, he's the prize — the only tight end whose athletic profile projects to a legitimate fantasy difference-maker. The ascending production suggests he's still improving, and the mismatch potential gives him a weekly ceiling that most tight ends can't match. In TE-premium leagues, he's worth a second-round pick. In standard formats, he's worth a third-round pick as the tight end who can end your streaming nightmare.

The positional scarcity is what drives his value. In dynasty leagues, tight end is the hardest position to fill — there are maybe 5-6 tight ends who produce consistent TE1 numbers, and the rest are streaming options. Sadiq has the athletic profile to join that top tier if he develops his route tree. That potential is worth paying for, even in a weak class.

The ascending production (2→24→36 catches, 0→2→5 TDs) is the strongest signal in his profile. That trajectory suggests he's just beginning to unlock his potential — he's not a finished product, he's a developing one. If the development continues at the NFL level, he could be catching 60-70 passes per season within three years, which would make him a top-5 dynasty TE.
sadiq.badgersFit: Sadiq is the tight end you draft to stop streaming the position forever. The 6'6" frame with basketball athleticism is a red-zone cheat code — he'll catch touchdowns that other tight ends can't because he wins above the rim. At pick 2.16 he's a bargain for a potential TE1. The ascending production (2→24→36 catches) means he's just getting started, and the athletic profile suggests the ceiling is much higher than what he's shown so far. If you're tired of rotating tight ends based on matchups, Sadiq is your exit ticket. Draft him and enjoy the positional advantage.
sadiq.verdict: Top dynasty TE in this class. Athletic mismatch with ascending production.

simpson.shortReport: The most accurate quarterback in this class, and it's not particularly close. Simpson's 71.4% completion rate and 28-4 TD-INT ratio in his first starting season at Alabama are the kind of numbers that make NFL scouts take notice — not because the raw numbers are impressive, but because the tape confirms they're real. He puts the ball in stride consistently, processes the field quickly, and gets the ball out before the rush arrives. The five rushing touchdowns are the fantasy hook that elevates him from 'accurate pocket passer' to 'viable fantasy QB1 with a floor.' The risk is that it's one year of tape surrounded by NFL-caliber weapons at Alabama — we're betting on the development, not confirming it.
simpson.athleticProfile: Simpson is a functional athlete whose mobility shows up in the pocket rather than on designed runs. He's not going to run a 4.6 at the combine or execute a 10-carry read-option game plan, but he moves well enough to extend plays, climb the pocket, and convert third-and-medium with his legs when the defense gives him a lane. The 62-287-5TD rushing line in 2025 is modest in volume but efficient in execution — he's not running for yards, he's running for first downs and touchdowns. That distinction matters for fantasy because it means his rushing production is concentrated in high-value situations (goal-line and third-down conversions) rather than spread across low-value scramble yards. His mechanics are clean — he sets his feet, drives from his core, and delivers the ball with a quick, compact release that minimizes the time between decision and throw. That mechanical efficiency is what allows him to post a 2.3-second time-to-throw, which is among the best in this class.
strengths: The accuracy is elite and it manifests in multiple ways that matter for fantasy. First, he puts the ball in stride — receivers don't have to break stride or reach back to catch his passes, which means they can transition into after-the-catch mode immediately. That adds yards to every completion and touchdowns to every red-zone opportunity. Second, he's accurate on the move — when he bails the pocket and throws on the run, he maintains his platform and delivers a catchable ball. That's rare for a pocket-passer type and it's what allows him to create off-schedule plays that generate fantasy points. Third, he's accurate in the red zone — he doesn't force balls into tight windows, he places them where only his receiver can make the play. That precision is what produced 28 touchdowns against just 4 interceptions.

The TD-INT ratio is remarkable not just for the raw numbers but for what it reveals about his decision-making process. A 28-4 ratio in a first-year starting season suggests a quarterback who processes information quickly and makes correct decisions at a high rate. He's not holding the ball waiting for the perfect throw — he's identifying the open receiver, delivering the ball on time, and moving on to the next play. That efficiency is the foundation of consistent fantasy production because it means he'll generate scoring opportunities without the catastrophic turnover weeks that sink fantasy lineups.

The functional mobility gives him a fantasy floor that pure pocket passers lack. Five rushing touchdowns in a season is a legitimate scoring contribution — it's the equivalent of an extra 300 passing yards and 3 passing touchdowns spread across the season. In a bad passing week where he throws for 180 yards and one touchdown, the rushing touchdown alone can elevate him to a QB1 line. That floor is what makes him startable in single-QB leagues and valuable in superflex — you're not benching him because he had a bad passing day, because his legs provide a safety net.

The Alabama pedigree means he's been developed in an NFL-style program with NFL-caliber coaching and NFL-caliber weapons. He's been in the building with Nick Saban and Kalen DeBoer, he's practiced against NFL-caliber defenses, and he's thrown to NFL-caliber receivers. That preparation matters because it means the transition to the NFL won't be as jarring as it is for quarterbacks from smaller programs. He's already been coached on protections, audibles, and game management — things that other quarterbacks in this class are still learning.
weaknesses: The one-year sample is the biggest risk in his profile. Simpson attempted 29 passes total in 2023-24 as Jalen Milroe's backup. His entire starting resume is one season at Alabama. That's not enough to confirm that the accuracy and decision-making are sustainable traits rather than a single-season peak. The risk is that the 2025 season represents his ceiling — a perfect storm of elite weapons, favorable matchups, and a system that maximized his strengths — and that the real Simpson is closer to a 22-8 TD-INT quarterback with 65% accuracy. That's still a viable NFL starter, but it's not a fantasy QB1.

The Alabama talent inflation is impossible to ignore. He was throwing to future NFL receivers, running behind future NFL offensive linemen, and playing against defenses that had to respect the run game because Alabama's running backs were also future NFL players. Every quarterback looks better when surrounded by elite talent. The question is whether Simpson can elevate a lesser roster the way he elevated Alabama's already-elite roster. We don't have evidence that he can because we've never seen him try.

The arm strength is good but not elite. He can make every throw in the intermediate game — outs, digs, seams, corner routes — but he struggles to drive the ball on deep outs to the boundary and his deep ball loses velocity when he has to throw it on a line rather than with touch. That limitation won't prevent him from being a productive NFL quarterback, but it does cap his ceiling as a fantasy asset because it limits the number of 40+ yard completions he can generate.

The 28 touchdowns may represent a peak that regresses significantly in the NFL. Alabama scored on 52% of its red-zone possessions in 2025, which is an elite rate that reflects the overall talent of the offense. In the NFL, even the best red-zone offenses score on about 60% of possessions. If Simpson lands on a team with a less efficient red-zone offense, his touchdown total could drop to 18-20, which would make him a QB2 rather than a QB1 in fantasy.
simpson.nflFit:
Simpson is best in a West Coast or play-action system that emphasizes accuracy, timing, and efficient decision-making. He's not a system-breaker who can carry an offense with his arm talent or his legs — he's a system-executor who thrives when the scheme creates open receivers and he delivers the ball on time. The rushing touchdowns make him a fantasy asset even if the passing volume is moderate. The ideal fit is a team with a run-heavy identity that uses play-action to create clean pockets and open windows — think Atlanta, Tennessee, or Pittsburgh.

The system dependency is the key variable in his fantasy projection. In a well-designed offense that creates schemed openings and play-action opportunities, Simpson will produce efficient numbers because he processes quickly and delivers accurately. In a broken offense with a bad line and no running game, his limitations — modest arm strength, lack of off-schedule creativity — will be exposed. He's not the kind of quarterback who can elevate bad situations.

The rushing touchdowns are the fantasy insurance policy. Even in a bad offensive environment, Simpson will produce 4-5 rushing TDs per season because he's willing to run near the goal line and he's effective at it. That floor makes him startable in superflex regardless of the passing situation.
simpson.comp:
The best comparison is Brock Purdy with functional mobility — an accurate, efficient quarterback who maximizes the talent around him and produces fantasy points through precision rather than explosiveness. Like Purdy, Simpson's fantasy value depends on the quality of his supporting cast. The floor comparison is Davis Mills — an accurate pocket passer who produces modest numbers because he lacks the arm talent or mobility to create outside of structure.

The Purdy comparison is about the system-executor profile specifically. Purdy has thrived in San Francisco because the scheme creates open receivers and he delivers the ball accurately on schedule. Simpson has the same skill set — he's not going to create outside of structure, but he'll execute at a high level when the structure is sound. The difference is that Purdy has better arm strength and more off-schedule ability than Simpson has shown.

The Mills comparison is the floor if the system doesn't support him. Mills has been a competent but unspectacular quarterback in Houston because the talent around him is limited and the scheme doesn't create easy completions. Simpson could fall into a similar trap if he lands on a team with a bad offensive line and no running game. The rushing touchdowns provide some insulation, but they can't fully compensate for a bad passing environment.
simpson.dynastyOutlook:
Simpson is the QB2 in this class for dynasty purposes, behind Mendoza. The 28-4 TD-INT ratio and 71.4% accuracy are elite signals, but the one-year sample and Alabama talent inflation are real risks. The five rushing touchdowns give him a weekly QB1 floor that makes him startable even in bad passing weeks. In superflex, he's worth a late-first or early-second round pick. In single-QB, he's worth a mid-round pick as a QB1 with a safe floor.

The gap between Simpson and Mendoza is the rushing ceiling. Mendoza projects to 6-8 rushing TDs per season; Simpson projects to 4-5. That difference doesn't sound like much, but over a 17-game season it's the difference between a QB5 and a QB8 finish. Simpson makes up some of that gap with superior accuracy and decision-making, but the rushing floor is the tiebreaker in Mendoza's favor.

The Alabama talent inflation is the discount factor. Simpson was throwing to NFL-caliber receivers in a system designed by an elite coordinator. In the NFL, he won't have those advantages. Expect regression to 20-22 passing TDs and 4-5 rushing TDs as a realistic rookie baseline, which is still a QB1 in superflex but not the elite producer his college numbers suggest.
simpson.badgersFit:
Simpson is the QB2 in this class and a legitimate franchise quarterback option if you miss on Mendoza. The 71.4% accuracy and 28-4 TD-INT ratio are elite, but the one-year sample at Alabama surrounded by NFL weapons is a real risk. The five rushing touchdowns are the fantasy differentiator — they give him a weekly floor that makes him startable even when the passing game is struggling. At pick 2.13 he's worth it if you need a quarterback. Pair him with a RB/WR in the first round and you have a championship-caliber foundation. Just don't pay for the 28-TD ceiling — expect 20-22 TDs and 5-6 rushing TDs, which is still a QB1.

The Badgers-specific value is the accuracy floor. In a dynasty league where most quarterbacks are volatile week-to-week, Simpson's precision gives him a higher weekly floor than his raw numbers suggest. He'll complete 65-68% of his passes, which means he's moving the chains and sustaining drives even when the big plays aren't there. That consistency is valuable in superflex where you need a reliable QB2.

The risk is the Alabama premium. You're paying for production that was inflated by elite weapons and scheme. If Simpson lands in a bad offensive environment, the accuracy could decline and the rushing TDs might not be enough to keep him startable. At pick 2.13, you're paying for the ceiling — but the floor is acceptable because of the rushing contribution.
simpson.verdict: High-floor QB2 with QB1 upside via rushing TDs. One-year risk is real.

cooper.shortReport: One of the most encouraging developmental arcs in this class. Cooper went from an afterthought (12 catches in 2023) to a go-to weapon (74 catches, 9 TDs in 2025) through genuine improvement in his route-running, his release package, and his understanding of leverage against defensive coverage. The production ascent is steep enough to be a strong signal — every metric improved every year — but the one-elite-year concern and the Indiana spread-system context introduce enough uncertainty to keep him out of the top tier. He's a WR3 with WR2 upside if the development continues at the NFL level.
cooper.athleticProfile: Cooper is a smooth athlete whose game is built on efficiency rather than explosiveness. He doesn't have the sudden burst that creates instant separation at the stem top — his breaks are deliberate rather than violent, and he wins by building speed through the route rather than snapping open at the break point. That profile works well on vertical routes and deep comebacks where he can use his long speed and tracking ability to win downfield, but it limits him on short-area routes where suddenness is the separator. His after-the-catch ability is rooted in size and lean rather than elusiveness — he breaks arm tackles with his frame and keeps his legs driving through contact, but he doesn't make defenders miss in space. The 18.3 YPR in 2023 and 16.2 in 2024 reflect his ability to win on vertical concepts; the decline to 12.6 in 2025 reflects the shift from big-play efficiency to volume-based production as his role expanded.
strengths: The production trajectory is the strongest signal in his profile. Going from 12 catches to 45 to 74 — with touchdowns climbing from 2 to 7 to 9 — isn't just incremental improvement. It's a player who transformed from a depth piece into the focal point of a Big Ten passing game. That kind of development arc suggests genuine skill growth rather than just increased opportunity. He didn't just get more targets — he earned them by becoming a better receiver. The three Big Ten WR of the Week awards in 2025 confirm that the production was consistent and recognized, not just a few lucky games.

The deep-ball tracking and body positioning are his most translatable receiving skills. He wins downfield not by running past corners but by positioning his body between the defender and the ball, then adjusting to the throw with the kind of late hands and body control that make him a reliable target on 50/50 balls. He's not as dominant at the catch point as Tate — he doesn't have the same leaping timing or hand strength — but he's effective because he creates enough separation with his positioning that the catch point becomes 60/40 rather than 50/50.

The red-zone production improved significantly as his route-running developed. Nine touchdowns on 74 catches in 2025 is a solid rate that reflects his ability to win with size and positioning in tight spaces. He's not a jump-ball specialist — he wins by finding the soft spot in zone coverage and presenting a clean target for his quarterback. That skill translates to the NFL because it's rooted in spatial awareness and timing, not just physical dominance.

The reliability is an underappreciated dimension of his game. He catches the ball consistently, runs clean routes that his quarterback can trust, and shows up every week. In fantasy, that reliability translates to a solid PPR floor — he'll give you 5-6 catches for 60-70 yards most weeks, which is a WR3 line that won't tank your lineup.
weaknesses: The lack of suddenness in his breaks is the primary concern for his NFL projection. When he faces NFL corners who can mirror his route stem and recover from the break point, he may struggle to create the consistent separation that made him productive at Indiana. His game is built on building speed through the route rather than snapping open at the top, which works against college corners who can't match his stride-for-stride but may not work against NFL corners who can.

The Indiana spread system created schemed openings that inflated his production. The Hoosiers' offense uses motion, bunch formations, and RPO concepts to create clean releases and open windows for receivers. Cooper benefited from these schematic advantages — he wasn't always winning against tight man coverage; he was running into space that the scheme created. NFL defenses will take away those easy completions and force him to win on traditional route concepts against tighter coverage.

The YPR decline (18.3→16.2→12.6) is a natural consequence of increased volume, but it also reveals the ceiling on his big-play ability. As his role expanded from deep-ball specialist to volume receiver, his efficiency declined because he was running more short and intermediate routes that don't generate the same yards-per-catch. That's fine — it's a normal development arc — but it tells you that his ceiling is as a possession-and-move-the-chains receiver rather than a big-play weapon.

The one-elite-year concern applies here too. His 2023 and 2024 seasons were modest — 12 and 45 catches respectively. The 74-catch breakout in 2025 could represent genuine development, or it could represent a system and opportunity peak. The truth is probably somewhere in between, but the risk is that the 2025 version of Cooper is his ceiling rather than his floor.
cooper.nflFit:
Cooper is a Z-receiver in a spread offense that uses motion and formationing to create favorable matchups. He needs a quarterback who throws with anticipation and an offensive coordinator who uses schemed openings to get him clean releases. He's not a boundary winner against press-man — he needs to be put in motion or aligned in reduced splits where his route-running can shine without the physical battle at the line. The ideal fit is a team with a timing-based quarterback and a spread passing game — think Indianapolis, Denver, or Tampa Bay.

The motion dependency is the key factor in his projection. Cooper needs free releases to win — he can't fight through press coverage and create separation against physical corners. A coordinator who uses pre-snap motion, bunch formations, and reduced splits to get him clean releases will maximize his route-running ability. A coordinator who lines him up static on the boundary and asks him to win against press will expose his limitations.

The timing-based quarterback fit is equally important. Cooper's routes are precise but not sudden — he builds speed through the stem rather than snapping open at the break. That means he needs a quarterback who throws with anticipation, delivering the ball as he breaks rather than waiting for him to come open. A timing-based passer will maximize Cooper's separation; a hold-the-ball quarterback will waste it.
cooper.comp:
The best comparison is Michael Pittman — a smooth, reliable receiver whose production trajectory improved steadily through college and whose NFL value is rooted in volume and efficiency rather than explosiveness. Like Pittman, Cooper will give you consistent production without the boom weeks that define elite fantasy receivers. The floor comparison is Darius Slayton — a productive college receiver whose NFL role has been limited by a lack of elite traits.

The Pittman comparison is about the volume-dependent profile. Pittman has been a productive fantasy receiver because he gets targets — he's not a separator or a big-play threat, but he catches the ball consistently and produces 65-80 catches per season. Cooper projects to a similar arc if he lands in the right offense. The difference is that Pittman has more size and physicality, which gives him a higher floor against press coverage.

The Slayton comparison is the floor if the landing spot doesn't support him. Slayton has produced in the NFL but his role has been inconsistent because he lacks the elite traits that coordinators design around. Cooper could fall into a similar trap — productive in the right situation, but limited by a lack of suddenness and physicality that prevents him from being a featured weapon.
cooper.dynastyOutlook:
Cooper is a WR3/4 with weekly WR2 upside if he continues his development trajectory. The ascending production is the strongest signal in his profile, but the one-elite-year risk and the system inflation concern are real. In PPR, his volume-based profile gives him a solid floor. In standard, the lack of big-play ability caps his ceiling. His long-term value depends on whether the 2025 development arc continues at the NFL level or plateaus.

The development trajectory is the key variable. If Cooper continues improving at the NFL level — refining his breaks, expanding his route tree, adding suddenness to his stem — he could become a WR2 in PPR formats. If his development plateaus, he's a WR4/5 whose volume depends on the coordinator and the system. The ascending college production (12→45→74 catches) suggests the improvement is real, but the Indiana system context introduces enough uncertainty to temper expectations.

The PPR premium is significant. Cooper's profile is built on volume rather than explosiveness, which means he's more valuable in formats that reward catches. In standard scoring, his lack of big-play ability and modest TD rate cap him at WR4/5. In PPR, 65-70 catches gives him a usable floor even without the boom weeks.
cooper.badgersFit:
Cooper is a solid WR3 with WR2 upside if his development continues. At pick 2.30 he's great value — the ascending production (12→45→74 catches) is a strong signal that he's still improving, and the reliability means he won't tank your week. Pair him with a WR1 like Tate or Tyson and you're set at receiver. Don't expect the 18.3 YPR version — that's gone with volume. But 70 catches and 800-900 yards is a realistic NFL projection that makes him a solid WR3 in PPR formats.

The Badgers-specific value is the reliability at a discount price. At pick 2.30, you're getting a player whose floor is a productive PPR WR3 and whose ceiling is a WR2 if the development continues. That's excellent value for a late second-round pick — most players in this range have much wider ranges of outcomes.

The risk is the system inflation. Indiana's spread offense created schemed openings that Cooper won't get in the NFL. If his route-running isn't good enough to win against tighter coverage, the volume could collapse. But at pick 2.30, you're not paying for the ceiling — you're paying for the development trajectory and accepting the floor.
cooper.verdict: Ascending WR with solid floor and development trajectory. Worth the pick.

lane.shortReport: A red-zone cheat code whose entire fantasy value is tied to what he does near the goal line. Lane's 6'4" frame and massive catch radius make him a jump-ball specialist who turns contested catches into touchdowns at an absurd rate — 12 scores on just 46 catches in 2024. But when the offense isn't in scoring position, Lane essentially disappears from the game plan. He's never caught 50 balls in a season, his route tree is limited to go-balls, fades, and slants, and his production is almost entirely touchdown-dependent. In PPR formats, that dependency is a fatal flaw; in TD-heavy scoring, it's a superpower.
strengths: The frame and catch radius are his entire identity and they're elite. At 6'4" with long arms and strong hands, Lane creates a catching zone that most corners simply can't contest. He doesn't need separation to catch the ball — he needs the quarterback to throw it in his general direction and he'll go get it. That skill is scheme-proof and quarterback-proof in the red zone, where the field compresses and windows tighten. A quarterback doesn't need to make a perfect throw; he just needs to throw it up and let Lane out-jump the defender.

The 2024 touchdown rate (12 on 46 catches, 26.1%) was absurd and unsustainable, but it reveals a real skill: Lane knows how to finish. He doesn't just catch the ball in the end zone — he positions his body to shield the defender, times his jump to catch the ball at its highest point, and comes down with the ball secured. That finishing ability is rooted in spatial awareness and body control, not just size, which means it will translate to the NFL even as the rate regresses.

The improved YPR from 11.4 to 17.2 in 2025 is an encouraging development that suggests he's adding downfield ability to his red-zone game. If he can become a vertical threat in addition to a goal-line specialist, his fantasy floor improves because he'll produce yards even in weeks where he doesn't score.
weaknesses: The touchdown dependency is the defining limitation of his fantasy profile. When Lane doesn't score — which will happen in most weeks because even elite red-zone receivers score in roughly half their games — he gives you 3 catches for 40 yards, which is a WR6 line in PPR. That boom/bust profile is tolerable on your bench but unstartable as a flex play unless you're chasing ceiling.

The route tree is severely limited. He runs go-balls, fades, back-shoulder throws, and slants — that's essentially four routes. He doesn't run curl-stop, comeback, dig, or post-dig concepts that would expand his role beyond the red zone and the deep boundary. Until he develops a more complete route tree, he's a situational player whose snap count will be limited to obvious passing downs and red-zone packages.

The touchdown rate will regress dramatically. Even the best red-zone receivers in the NFL score on roughly 12-15% of their catches. Lane's 26.1% rate in 2024 was nearly double that. Expect 6-8 touchdowns per season going forward, which is still productive but changes his fantasy profile from 'elite TD scorer' to 'good TD scorer with terrible volume.'
lane.nflFit:
Red-zone specialist X-receiver in a vertical offense that needs a jump-ball target near the goal line. He's not an every-down receiver — he's a situational weapon whose value is concentrated inside the 20. The ideal fit is a team with a quarterback who excels at throwing 50/50 balls and an offense that already has its chain-mover and deep threat identified.

The situational role is the key constraint on his fantasy value. Lane will play roughly 40-50% of offensive snaps — red-zone packages, obvious passing downs, and vertical shot plays. That snap count limits his target volume to roughly 50-60 per season, which caps his PPR floor regardless of his efficiency. He needs a coordinator who will design specific red-zone plays for him rather than just hoping he wins in the flow of the offense.

The quarterback fit is critical. Lane needs a passer who throws with touch and timing on 50/50 balls — a quarterback who will throw it up and let Lane go get it. A fastball thrower who expects receivers to be open will waste Lane's best skill. The best fit is a quarterback like Justin Herbert or Joe Burrow who layers the deep ball with touch.
lane.comp:
Mike Williams in his Chargers prime — a big, TD-dependent boundary receiver whose production fluctuates with his quarterback's ability to get him the ball in scoring position. Like Williams, Lane will have weeks where he scores twice and weeks where he vanishes.

The Williams comparison is about the boom/bust profile specifically. Williams was one of the most volatile fantasy receivers in football — he'd give you 5-120-2 weeks and follow them with 2-28-0 weeks. Lane has the same profile because his production is rooted in contested catches and red-zone targets rather than consistent separation. The variance is structural, not situational.
lane.dynastyOutlook:
WR4/5 with weekly WR2 upside in TD-heavy scoring formats. In PPR, the low volume caps his floor and makes him unstartable in most weeks. His value is entirely concentrated in touchdown-dependent formats where his red-zone dominance translates to consistent scoring.

The weekly variance is the defining feature of his profile. Lane will win you 2-3 weeks per season with multi-TD games and be invisible the other 14 weeks. That profile makes him a better best-ball pick than a weekly-lineup asset, because in best-ball his boom weeks count without his bust weeks hurting you.

The TD regression is coming. Even the best red-zone receivers in the NFL score on roughly 12-15% of their catches. Lane's 26.1% rate in 2024 was nearly double that. Expect 6-8 TDs per season going forward, which is still productive but changes his profile from 'elite TD scorer' to 'good TD scorer with terrible volume.'
lane.badgersFit:
Lane is a TD-or-bust WR whose massive frame makes him a red-zone weapon but whose 47-catch volume makes him a PPR headache. At pick 3.80 he's a fine dart throw if you already have your WR1/2 locked in — he'll win you 2-3 weeks a year with multi-TD games and be invisible the rest. Don't reach for the 2024 TD rate; it's coming down hard. Draft him as your WR5 and enjoy the boom weeks when they come.

The Badgers-specific value is the complement to your PPR floor receivers. If you've already drafted Lemon and Cooper, Lane gives you a completely different skill set — a boom/bust TD specialist who can win weeks when your floor receivers are quiet. That diversity of profiles makes your roster more resilient.

The risk is the volume. Lane has never caught 50 balls in a season and his route tree is limited. He's a situational player whose snap count will be capped in the NFL. At pick 3.80, you're paying for the TD ceiling and accepting the floor.
lane.verdict: TD-dependent red-zone specialist. Low PPR floor, high TD ceiling.

singleton.shortReport: A touchdown scorer whose efficiency is declining in every measurable way. Singleton's 45 career rushing touchdowns are the headline — he's found the end zone 12+ times in three of four seasons at Penn State — but the underlying numbers tell a concerning story: yards per carry declining every year, receiving production collapsing from 41 catches in 2023 to 11 in 2025, and a split-backfield role that he never escaped. The 2023 receiving tape shows a three-down skill that no longer appears to exist. He's a two-down committee back whose fantasy value is almost entirely dependent on goal-line opportunities.
strengths: The touchdown production is real and it's rooted in a specific skill: patience near the goal line. Singleton doesn't try to run over defenders at the one-yard line — he reads the blocking scheme, identifies the soft shoulder, and squeezes through gaps that don't look open from the broadcast angle. That patience translates to any level because it's rooted in vision and spatial awareness, not just power. Twelve-plus rushing touchdowns in three of four seasons is a consistent demonstration of that skill.

The 2023 receiving tape (41-362-1TD) is a ghost that haunts his evaluation. In that season, Singleton ran actual route concepts out of the backfield — angle routes, swing screens, even a few wheel routes — and caught 41 passes. That's a three-down skill that would make him a PPR asset if it still existed. The problem is that it doesn't appear to exist anymore, but the tape is still on file and some team may believe they can resurrect it.

Four years of Power 5 production means he's experienced and reliable. He knows how to pass protect (improved significantly from 2023 to 2025), he understands blitz pickups, and he's been through the grind of a Big Ten schedule. That experience matters for NFL readiness even if the athletic profile is declining.
weaknesses: The declining efficiency is the most concerning trend in his profile. His YPC has dropped every year: 6.8→5.2→6.5→4.5. The 6.5 in 2024 is an outlier that came against a favorable schedule, but the overall trend is unmistakable — he's becoming less efficient as he accumulates mileage and faces better defenses. In the NFL, where the defensive talent is uniformly elite, that decline is likely to continue.

The receiving production collapse is the death knell for his PPR value. Going from 41 catches in 2023 to 24 in 2024 to 11 in 2025 isn't a dip — it's a collapse. Either Penn State stopped using him as a receiver (scheme decision), or he stopped producing as a receiver (skill decline). Either way, the result is the same: he's a two-down runner who comes off the field on third-and-long, which caps his snap count and his fantasy floor in PPR formats.

The split-backfield role raises questions about whether he can carry a full NFL workload. He's never been the solo guy — he's always shared carries with another back. That could mean he's best in a committee, or it could mean Penn State didn't trust him with a full load. NFL teams will see it the same way, which means he's likely to land in another committee rather than as a feature back.
The mileage is substantial: 634 career carries plus receiving work over four seasons. That's a lot of tread for a back whose efficiency is already declining.
singleton.nflFit:
Committee back with goal-line value in a power-run scheme. Singleton's best role is as the short-yardage and goal-line specialist in a backfield that already has a speed/receiving complement. He's not a three-down back — the receiving decline eliminates that possibility — but he can score touchdowns in a defined role.

The specific role matters because Singleton's fantasy value is entirely dependent on goal-line opportunities. In a power-run offense that runs the ball inside the 10-yard line, Singleton will score 8-10 TDs per season because his patience and vision near the goal line are genuine skills. In a spread offense that throws in the red zone, his TD opportunities will be limited and his fantasy value collapses.

The receiving decline means he comes off the field on third-and-long, which caps his snap count at roughly 50-60% of offensive plays. That snap count is enough for fantasy relevance if the TDs are there, but it eliminates any PPR upside.
singleton.comp:
Jamaal Williams — a reliable TD scorer with limited receiving upside who operates best as part of a committee. Like Williams, Singleton's fantasy value is concentrated in touchdown-dependent formats where his goal-line ability translates to consistent scoring.

The Williams comparison is about the TD-dependent profile specifically. Williams had a 17-TD season in Detroit because the Lions ran the ball near the goal line and Williams was the finisher. Singleton could have a similar spike season if he lands in the right offense. The difference is that Williams was a better receiver than Singleton's current version, which gave him more PPR appeal.
singleton.dynastyOutlook:
RB3/4 with weekly RB2 upside if he lands in a TD-friendly offense. The receiving decline is devastating for PPR value — he's essentially unstartable in PPR formats unless he scores. In standard scoring, the touchdown floor gives him flex appeal.

The range of outcomes is wide but skewed toward the downside. Best case: he lands in a power-run offense as the goal-line specialist and scores 10+ TDs, making him an RB2 in standard. Worst case: he lands in a spread offense that throws in the red zone and he scores 4-5 TDs with 11 catches, making him an RB5. The landing spot determines everything.

The PPR penalty is severe. With only 11 catches in 2025 and a declining receiving role, Singleton provides almost no PPR floor. In weeks where he doesn't score a TD, he'll give you 40-50 yards on 12 carries with 0 catches — a 5-point floor that sinks your lineup. He's only startable in standard or TD-heavy formats.
singleton.badgersFit:
Singleton is a TD-dependent RB whose best receiving days are behind him. The 45 career TDs are enticing, but the declining YPC (6.8→4.5) and collapsing receiving (41→11 catches) are red flags that can't be ignored. At pick 5.165 he's a fine dart — he'll score TDs in the right role but won't give you much else. Best as an RB3/flex play in standard scoring. In PPR, the 11 catches make him nearly unstartable. The 2023 receiving tape is a mirage.

The Badgers-specific value is the TD floor at a discount price. At pick 5.165, you're spending a minimal asset on a player who could score 8-10 TDs in the right landing spot. That's the kind of dart throw that wins dynasty leagues when it hits. The key is managing expectations — Singleton is not your RB2, he's your RB3/4 who provides TD upside in standard formats.

The declining efficiency is the real concern. A back whose YPC has dropped from 6.8 to 4.5 over four years is trending in the wrong direction, and NFL defenses will be the toughest he's faced. The 2023 receiving tape is tempting but the 2025 version of Singleton is the one you're drafting — and that version catches 11 balls.
singleton.verdict: TD scorer with declining efficiency. RB3 floor, RB2 ceiling in TD-friendly spots.

fields.shortReport: The definition of a steady, reliable possession receiver who will never wow you but will never disappear. Fields posted three consecutive 800-yard seasons across two programs — Virginia and Notre Dame — with remarkably consistent production (55-811, 55-808, 56-805). His 14.4-14.7 YPR shows he can win downfield with tracking and body control, not just underneath. The consistency is his calling card; the capped ceiling is his limitation. As a grad transfer, he's older than most prospects, which reduces his dynasty upside but increases his NFL readiness.
strengths: The consistency across two programs is remarkable and it speaks to a receiver whose skills translate regardless of system or quarterback. At Virginia, he produced with a spread-option quarterback. At Notre Dame, he produced with a pro-style quarterback. The numbers are nearly identical — 55-811 became 56-805 — which tells you the production is player-driven, not system-driven. That's the kind of reliability that NFL coaches value because it means they can count on him to be where he's supposed to be and catch the ball when it arrives.

The downfield ability (14.4-14.7 YPR) is an underappreciated dimension of his game because his production profile suggests a possession receiver. But he wins downfield with tracking and body control — he adjusts to the ball in flight, positions his body between the defender and the throw, and makes the catch without losing momentum. That's not a deep-speed skill; it's a ball-tracking skill, and it translates to the NFL because it's rooted in spatial awareness rather than athleticism.

The hands are reliable in a way that builds quarterback trust. He catches everything in his frame with natural pluck-and-tuck technique, and he catches in traffic without flinching. Drops are rare. That reliability is the foundation of his PPR floor — he converts targets into catches at a high rate, which means he'll produce even when the volume is modest.
weaknesses: He's not sudden or explosive, which caps his ceiling at every level. He doesn't create instant separation at the stem top, he doesn't make defenders miss after the catch, and he doesn't stress defenses vertically with pure speed. He wins with technique and size, which is effective but limited — there's a ceiling on how good a receiver can be when he's not explosive.

The age is a dynasty concern. As a grad transfer, he's already 23-24 years old, which means he's entering the NFL at an age when most receivers are entering their second or third season. That reduces his long-term developmental upside and makes him a win-now asset rather than a keeper piece.

He's never had a true breakout — 800 yards is good but not great. Every season looks the same, which is both a strength (consistency) and a weakness (no ceiling). In dynasty, you're drafting him for what he is, not for what he could become.
fields.nflFit:
Possession Z-receiver in a timing-based offense. His consistency and reliability make him a safe NFL starter who will produce at a moderate level regardless of situation. He'll never be a star, but he'll be a productive professional for 6-8 years.

The scheme-proof nature of his game is what makes him a safe NFL projection. Fields has produced the same numbers at Virginia and Notre Dame, with different quarterbacks and different systems. That consistency suggests his production is player-driven, not system-driven, which means he'll produce at a moderate level regardless of where he lands.

The age is the limiting factor for his NFL ceiling. As a grad transfer, he's entering the league at 23-24, which means his developmental window is shorter than most rookies. He's closer to his finished product than a 21-year-old prospect, which means what you see is what you get.
fields.comp:
Allen Robinson in his prime — consistent, reliable, not explosive. Like Robinson, Fields produces through technique and size rather than athleticism, which gives him a safe floor but a capped ceiling.

The Robinson comparison is about the possession-profile specifically. Robinson was one of the most reliable fantasy receivers in football for several years because he caught everything and produced consistent volume. Fields has a similar profile — he'll give you 55-60 catches for 800 yards with 5-6 TDs per season, which is a WR3 in PPR and a WR4 in standard. The difference is that Robinson had more size and physicality, which gave him a higher TD ceiling.
fields.dynastyOutlook:
WR4/5 with a safe floor. The consistency is appealing for win-now rosters, but the capped ceiling and older age make him a poor keeper asset. He's a reliable flex play who won't tank your week.

The win-now versus keeper distinction is important. Fields is a 23-24 year old rookie who's already near his ceiling — he's not going to develop into something he's never been. In a win-now dynasty format, that reliability has value because you know what you're getting. In a keeper league where you're building for the future, his age and capped ceiling make him less appealing than a younger player with more upside.

The PPR floor is his best attribute. Fields will catch 55-60 balls per season because he's always open by enough and he always catches the ball. That floor gives him a weekly PPR baseline of 8-10 points, which is a usable flex play in deep leagues.
fields.badgersFit:
Fields is the definition of steady — 800+ yards every year, never spectacular, never invisible. At pick 3.74 he's a solid WR4/5 who won't tank your week. The age (grad transfer) limits his dynasty upside — he's a win-now piece, not a keeper. Grab him if you need reliable WR depth, but don't expect him to become more than what he's already shown.

The Badgers-specific value is the floor at a reasonable price. At pick 3.74, you're getting a player whose worst-case scenario is 55 catches and 800 yards — that's a usable WR4 in PPR. Most players in this range have much wider ranges of outcomes, including the possibility of washing out entirely. Fields won't wash out because his production is player-driven, not system-driven.

The age is the discount factor. In a keeper league, you'd rather have a 21-year-old with upside than a 24-year-old with a known ceiling. But in a win-now format, Fields' reliability is an asset, not a liability.
fields.verdict: Consistent possession WR. Safe floor, capped ceiling.

branch.shortReport: A return specialist whose receiving production exploded after transferring from USC to Georgia. Branch's three return touchdowns in 2023 (2 KR, 1 PR) are the flashiest highlights on his early resume, but the real story is the 2025 breakout: 81 catches for 811 yards and 6 TDs at Georgia — a massive leap from his USC numbers (31-320-2 in 2023, 47-503-1 in 2024). The return touchdowns are fluky and don't translate to standard fantasy scoring, but the receiving volume spike at Georgia changes his profile from return-only to a potential PPR contributor. He's a return-league asset with emerging receiving value.
strengths: The return ability is genuine and it's rooted in a specific skill: elusiveness in space. Branch makes the first defender miss on kick and punt returns with the same hip-shift and acceleration that he shows after catches. That elusiveness translates to any level because it's rooted in body control, not just speed. In return-yard leagues, that skill has direct fantasy value.

The receiving production exploded in 2025 after transferring to Georgia. Going from 47 catches at USC to 81 at Georgia — with TD improvement (1→6) — shows he earned a featured role in the passing game. The jet-sweep versatility (4-63 in 2023) adds a gadget dimension that creative coordinators can exploit. The volume spike is the strongest signal in his profile: 81 catches at Georgia is legitimate WR1 volume.
weaknesses: The receiving volume spiked at Georgia but the efficiency is concerning. Eighty-one catches for 811 yards is just 10.0 YPR — that's possession receiver efficiency, not explosive playmaking. He's catching a lot of balls but not doing much with them after the catch, which caps his standard-league value even in PPR-friendly volume. His smaller frame limits his effectiveness against press and over the middle. The volume is real but the impact per touch is modest.

The USC-to-UGA transfer worked for volume but the efficiency tells a different story. His catches jumped from 47 to 81, but his YPR dropped from 10.7 to 10.0 and his yards only went from 503 to 811 — the volume increase accounts for most of the yardage gain. He's a good returner who became a high-volume but low-efficiency receiver at Georgia, which raises questions about whether the volume is sustainable in the NFL or was a product of Georgia's scheme.

Return touchdowns are fluky and unsustainable. Three return TDs in 2023 is exceptional, but the NFL average for return specialists is roughly one per season. Expect that number to regress to 0-1 per year, which eliminates the fantasy value that made him attractive in the first place.
branch.nflFit:
Slot receiver and returner in a spread offense. The return value gives him a roster floor even if the offense doesn't feature him as a receiver. He's a fourth or fifth receiver who contributes on special teams — a valuable NFL role but a limited fantasy one.

The return value is the roster-floor insurance. Even if Branch never develops as a receiver, he'll stick on an NFL roster because return specialists are valuable on special teams. That floor is important for dynasty because it means he won't be cut — he'll have a role even if it's not a fantasy-relevant one.

The receiving ceiling depends on whether the Georgia volume was real or scheme-driven. If an NFL team features him in the slot with schemed touches, he could produce 60-70 catches. If he's relegated to gadget plays, he's a return specialist only.
branch.comp:
Devin Duvernay — a return specialist with slot receiving ability whose fantasy value is almost entirely concentrated in return-yard leagues. Like Duvernay, Branch will have occasional receiving weeks but can't be relied upon as a consistent fantasy producer.

The Duvernay comparison is about the return-first profile. Duvernay has survived in the NFL because of his return ability, not his receiving production. Branch is in a similar position — his NFL value is as a returner who occasionally contributes as a slot receiver. The difference is that Branch's 2025 Georgia volume (81 catches) suggests more receiving upside than Duvernay ever showed in college.
branch.dynastyOutlook:
WR5/6 with return-yard value. In standard leagues he's a fringe roster player. In return-yard leagues he's a WR4 with upside when he breaks a return for a score.

The return-yard premium is the only thing that makes him rosterable in standard formats. In standard scoring, Branch is a fringe player who'll give you 50-60 catches for 550-650 yards with occasional gadget contributions. In return-yard leagues, he adds 500-800 return yards and the occasional return TD, which elevates him to a usable WR4/5.

The receiving upside is real but efficiency-dependent. Branch's catches jumped from 47 to 81 at Georgia and his TDs climbed from 1 to 6. If he maintains that volume in the NFL, he could become a WR4 in PPR formats. But the 10.0 YPR suggests he's a volume-dependent producer whose value evaporates if the targets dry up.
branch.badgersFit:
Branch is a return-league asset with emerging PPR value. The three return TDs in 2023 are flashy but not sustainable. At pick 3.79 he's worth it in PPR and return-yard leagues — his 81-811-6 receiving line at Georgia is legitimate volume. In standard formats the 10.0 YPR caps his value, but the catch volume gives him a PPR floor most return specialists lack.

The Badgers-specific value depends on your league settings. In return-yard leagues, Branch is a legitimate asset whose elusiveness in space translates to consistent return production. In standard PPR, he's a fringe roster player whose receiving volume gives him a floor but whose efficiency limits his ceiling.

The transfer result is mixed. Branch left USC for Georgia and the volume spiked (47→81 catches) but the efficiency barely improved (10.7→10.0 YPR). The volume spike is encouraging but it may be scheme-dependent rather than talent-driven. At pick 3.79, you're getting a player with legitimate PPR volume and return upside, but the low YPR means he's dependent on targets for value.
branch.verdict: Return-league specialist with emerging PPR value.

hurst.shortReport: The most productive small-school receiver in this draft class, and it's not close. Hurst's 31 touchdowns in three seasons at Georgia State are the Sun Belt all-time receiving TD record — a mark that reflects both his red-zone dominance and his ability to produce against defenses that knew he was the only threat on the field. His production climbed every year (822→1047→1202 yards, 7→11→13 TDs) while maintaining 15.5+ YPR, which tells you he was getting better even as defenses game-planned specifically to stop him. The competition jump from the Sun Belt to the NFL is the obvious concern, but the ascending production against increasingly focused defenses is a signal that his skills are real, not just a product of weak opponents.
strengths: The touchdown production is historic at any level. Thirty-one receiving touchdowns in three seasons means he's finding the end zone roughly once every five catches — a rate that reflects genuine finishing ability, not just volume. He wins in the red zone with a combination of size, body positioning, and strong hands that allows him to shield the ball from defenders and come down with it in traffic. That skill translates to the NFL because it's rooted in spatial awareness and hand strength, not just athletic dominance over lesser defenders.

The ascending production every year is a stronger signal than the raw numbers. He didn't just produce against Sun Belt defenses — he produced more each year despite being the obvious focal point of every defensive game plan. When you're the only threat on a Georgia State offense and you still improve from 822 to 1202 yards, you're creating your own opportunities. That's a player-driven skill, not a system-driven one.

The 74 catches in 2025 prove he can handle volume, not just big plays. Some small-school receivers post gaudy YPR numbers on 30 catches — they're specialists who can't handle a full route tree. Hurst caught 74 balls, which means he's running the full route tree and producing on short, intermediate, and deep concepts. That versatility matters for NFL projection because it means he's not a one-trick deep-ball specialist.
weaknesses: The competition jump is the obvious and legitimate concern. Sun Belt defensive backs aren't NFL-caliber — many of them are undersized or slow by Power 5 standards. Hurst's production came against defenders who couldn't match his size or speed, and the NFL will present a dramatically different challenge. The best-case scenario is that his skills translate because they're rooted in technique and spatial awareness; the worst-case scenario is that he can't separate against corners who can match his physical profile.

The route tree is likely limited because Georgia State's offense didn't require him to run the full spectrum of NFL routes. He dominated with size and speed on vertical concepts, fades, and slants — the kind of routes that work against overmatched defenders. The question is whether he can run curl-stop, comeback, dig, and option routes with the precision that NFL offenses demand. We won't know until he faces NFL-caliber coaching and competition.
The age is a concern — he'll be 24 as a rookie, which limits his developmental upside and makes him a win-now asset rather than a long-term keeper.
hurst.nflFit:
X-receiver in a vertical offense. The production is undeniable but the competition jump is real. Best case: he's a late-round WR3 who produces in the red zone. Worst case: he can't separate against NFL corners and becomes a practice-squad player.

The competition jump is the defining variable in his projection. Sun Belt defensive backs aren't NFL-caliber — many of them are undersized or slow by Power 5 standards. Hurst's production came against defenders who couldn't match his size or speed. The NFL will present a dramatically different challenge.

The red-zone skill is the most translatable element of his game. Even if his separation declines against better corners, his ability to shield the ball and finish in traffic should translate because it's rooted in spatial awareness and hand strength, not just athletic dominance.
hurst.comp:
Dominique Douglas — a small-school producer who translated his production to a solid NFL role. Like Douglas, Hurst's skills are real but the competition jump will determine whether he's a contributor or a camp body.

The Douglas comparison is about the small-school-to-NHL translation arc. Douglas produced at a high level in college against lesser competition and translated enough of that production to stick in the NFL. Hurst has a similar profile — the skills are real, but the question is whether they're good enough against NFL-caliber defenders. The red-zone finishing ability gives him a higher floor than most small-school prospects.
hurst.dynastyOutlook:
WR5/6 with WR3 upside if he lands in the right offense. The TD production is tantalizing but the competition jump is the risk. He's a late-round dart throw whose value depends entirely on whether his skills translate to NFL-caliber competition.

The range of outcomes is extremely wide. Best case: he lands in a vertical offense as a red-zone specialist and produces 50-700-8, making him a WR4 in TD-heavy formats. Worst case: he can't separate against NFL corners and becomes a camp body. That variance makes him a polarizing pick — some managers will love the TD production, others won't touch the small-school risk.

The TD production is the hook. Thirty-one receiving touchdowns in three seasons is historic at any level. Even with regression to 5-7 TDs per NFL season, that's a usable floor in TD-heavy formats where most WR5/6 types don't score at all.
hurst.badgersFit:
Hurst is my favorite late-round dart throw. 31 TDs in 3 seasons at any level is absurd — that kind of finishing ability doesn't disappear against better competition. At pick 3.84 he's worth the gamble. The Sun Belt competition is a fair concern, but he produced every year and got better every year despite being the focal point of every defensive game plan. He's your WR6 with WR3 upside. In a dynasty league, this is the kind of pick that wins you a championship if it hits.

The Badgers-specific value is the TD floor at a bargain price. At pick 3.84, you're spending a third-round pick on a player whose worst-case scenario is a camp body and whose best-case scenario is a WR3 who scores 8+ TDs per season. That's the kind of risk-reward profile that wins dynasty leagues when it connects.

The ascending production is the strongest signal. He didn't just produce against Sun Belt defenses — he produced more each year despite being the obvious focal point of every defensive game plan. That's a player-driven skill, not a system-driven one, and it suggests the TD production is real even if the efficiency regresses.
hurst.verdict: Small-school TD machine with ascending production. Late-round gem if he translates.

stribling.shortReport: A reliable possession receiver whose 2024 dip when the quarterback changed at Ole Miss is a red flag that NFL teams will scrutinize. Stribling's production rebounded in 2025 (54-800-6), but the fact that he disappeared when the QB situation was unsettled raises questions about whether he can produce independent of his offensive environment. In the NFL, where quarterback play fluctuates, that dependency could make him inconsistent.
strengths: The rebound in 2025 shows resilience. After a disappointing 2024 where his production collapsed with a new quarterback, Stribling came back with 54-800-6 in 2025 — proving that the 2024 dip was situational rather than a permanent decline. That resilience matters because it shows he can overcome adversity and produce even after a bad year.

He's a reliable possession receiver who catches everything in his frame. His hands are consistent and he runs clean intermediate routes that create just enough separation for his quarterback to find him. That reliability gives him a PPR floor in the right role.
weaknesses: The 2024 dip when the quarterback changed is the defining concern. In the NFL, quarterback situations change frequently — injuries, benchings, scheme changes. If Stribling can only produce with a comfortable, established quarterback, his fantasy value will fluctuate wildly based on factors he can't control. That dependency makes him a risky dynasty asset.

He's not a deep threat and never will be. His YPR is capped in the 12-13 range, which means he's a possession receiver whose fantasy value is entirely dependent on catch volume. In weeks where the volume is low, he'll give you 3-40, which is unstartable.
stribling.nflFit:
Possession Z-receiver in a timing-based offense that features intermediate routes. He needs a stable quarterback situation to produce consistently.

The quarterback dependency is the defining constraint on his fantasy value. Stribling's 2024 dip when the QB changed at Ole Miss shows he can't produce independent of his offensive environment. In the NFL, where quarterback situations change frequently, that dependency could make him inconsistent.

The possession profile means he needs volume to produce fantasy value. He's not going to score 10 TDs or rip off 50-yard catches — he needs 5-6 targets per game to give you a usable PPR floor. That volume depends on the quarterback trusting him, which circles back to the dependency concern.
stribling.comp:
Donte Moncrief — flashes of ability but inconsistent production tied to quarterback play.

The Moncrief comparison is about the QB-dependent profile specifically. Moncrief had weeks where he looked like a WR2 and weeks where he disappeared, and the variance was almost entirely tied to the quality of his quarterback play. Stribling has a similar profile — he'll produce when the QB is comfortable and struggle when the QB situation is unsettled.
stribling.dynastyOutlook:
WR6/7. Fringe roster player in 12-team leagues whose value fluctuates with his quarterback.

The QB dependency makes him nearly unrosterable in dynasty formats where you need consistent weekly production. He'll have 5-70 weeks when the passing game is clicking and 2-20 weeks when it's not, and you can't predict which version you'll get. That variance is tolerable on your bench but unstartable as a flex play.

The floor is low because his production is almost entirely dependent on external factors he can't control. In a good QB situation, he's a WR5. In a bad QB situation, he's off the roster. That dependency makes him a poor dynasty asset.
stribling.badgersFit:
Stribling is roster filler. The 2024 dip when the QB changed is a red flag — NFL QBs change too. At pick 4.33 he's overdrafted for his production. Better options available in this range.

The Badgers-specific concern is the QB dependency. In dynasty leagues, you need players who produce regardless of their environment. Stribling has shown he can't do that — he disappeared when the QB situation at Ole Miss changed. That dependency makes him a risky pick at any cost, but especially at pick 4.33 where better options are available.

The 2025 rebound is encouraging but doesn't erase the 2024 red flag. He proved he can bounce back, but he also proved he can collapse when the environment changes. In the NFL, environments change constantly.
stribling.verdict: Inconsistent possession WR with QB dependency. Limited dynasty appeal.

douglas.shortReport: A pure deep-ball specialist whose fantasy value is concentrated in the weeks where the vertical passing game connects. Douglas's 16.8 and 16.4 YPR in 2024-25 show he wins downfield with natural ball-tracking and body control, but his 52-catch volume and limited route tree mean he'll give you 2-3 boom weeks and vanish for the rest. He's a bench stash for boom-week hunting, not a reliable starter.
strengths: The deep-ball tracking is his most translatable skill. He adjusts to the ball in flight with natural hands and body control, making him a reliable target on go-balls and post routes even when the throw isn't perfect. That tracking ability translates to the NFL because it's rooted in hand-eye coordination and spatial awareness, not just speed.

The ascending production (11→40→52 catches) shows he's gradually earning a larger role. The improvement from 11 catches as a freshman to 52 as a junior suggests he's developing as a route runner, not just running faster than defenders.
weaknesses: He's essentially a one-dimensional deep-ball specialist. His route tree is limited to go-balls, posts, and occasional seam routes — he doesn't run the curl-stop, comeback, or option routes that would expand his role and increase his volume. Until he develops a more complete route tree, he's a situational deep threat whose snap count will be limited.

The volume ceiling is modest. Fifty-two catches in a season gives him a terrible PPR floor — he'll have weeks where he catches 1 ball for 30 yards and weeks where he catches 5 for 120 and a touchdown. That boom/bust profile makes him unstartable in most weeks.
douglas.nflFit:
Deep-ball Z-receiver in a vertical offense that takes shots downfield. He's a situational weapon whose value is concentrated in specific game scripts — when the offense is trailing and needs big plays, or when the defense is playing single-high safety.

The situational role caps his snap count. Douglas will play roughly 40-50% of offensive snaps — vertical shot plays, obvious passing downs, and trailing game scripts. That snap count limits his target volume to roughly 50-60 per season, which caps his PPR floor regardless of his efficiency.

The deep-ball tracking is the one skill that translates regardless of scheme. He adjusts to the ball in flight with natural hands and body control, which means he'll catch deep balls even when the throw isn't perfect. That skill is valuable in any vertical offense.
douglas.comp:
Mack Hollins — a deep-ball specialist with limited volume who produces in boom/bust fashion. Like Hollins, Douglas will win you 2-3 weeks a year and be invisible the rest.

The Hollins comparison is about the boom/bust deep-ball profile. Hollins has survived in the NFL because he can win on go-balls and post routes, but his volume is too low for consistent fantasy production. Douglas has a similar profile — he'll give you 4-120-1 weeks and 1-20-0 weeks with no way to predict which version you'll get.
douglas.dynastyOutlook:
WR5/6 with boom weeks. The YPR is enticing but the volume caps his floor. He's a bench stash for the weeks when the deep ball connects.

The weekly variance is extreme. Douglas will give you 2-3 boom weeks per season where he catches 4-5 balls for 100+ yards and a TD, and 14 quiet weeks where he catches 1-2 balls for 30 yards. That profile makes him a better best-ball pick than a weekly-lineup asset.

The ascending production (11→40→52 catches) is encouraging but the volume ceiling is still modest. Even if he improves to 60 catches, that's a WR4 in PPR with boom/bust variance. The ceiling is limited by his route tree.
douglas.badgersFit:
Douglas is a boom/bust WR5 with big-play ability. The 16+ YPR means when he catches it, it's a big gain, but 52 catches gives him a low PPR floor. At pick 4.75 he's a fine dart for your bench — don't start him unless you need a boom week. He'll either hit 2-3 big games or be off your roster by October.

The Badgers-specific value is the complement to your possession receivers. If you've drafted Lemon and Cooper for PPR floor, Douglas gives you a completely different profile — a boom/bust deep-ball specialist who can win weeks when your floor receivers are quiet. That diversity makes your roster more resilient.

The risk is the volume. Fifty-two catches in college doesn't project to consistent fantasy production in the NFL. He needs to expand his route tree to earn more targets, and there's no guarantee that development will happen.
douglas.verdict: Deep-ball specialist. Boom/bust WR5 for bench stash.

bernard.shortReport: A versatile but trait-limited receiver whose ascending production (41→50→60 catches) is more a product of opportunity and system than individual improvement. Bernard transferred from Washington to Alabama and produced modestly — 60 catches at Bama sounds impressive until you realize the Crimson Tide's offense creates schemed openings that inflate receiver production. He lacks the speed, size, or suddenness to create his own separation, which means his NFL role will be limited to depth and special teams.
strengths: The ascending production shows he's earning trust within the offense. Going from 41 to 60 catches across three seasons suggests coaches are finding more ways to use him — jet sweeps, return duties, and schemed touches that add hidden value. The kick-return contribution (8-167 in 2024) adds a dimension that gives him a roster floor even if the receiving production is modest.
The improved TD production (1→5→7) suggests he's finding ways to contribute in the red zone, even if it's through schemed touches rather than individual playmaking.
weaknesses: He lacks any standout trait — no elite speed, size, or suddenness that would create separation against NFL defensive backs. He's a jack-of-all-trades whose versatility is his calling card but whose lack of a defining skill caps his ceiling at every level.

The Alabama system inflated his production. The Crimson Tide's offense creates schemed openings — motion, bunch formations, RPOs — that generate clean releases and open windows for receivers. Bernard benefited from these schematic advantages, and his production may decline when he's not surrounded by elite talent.
bernard.nflFit:
Depth WR with return value. Best as a WR4/5 who contributes on special teams and provides versatile depth. He's a valuable NFL role player but a limited fantasy asset.

The return value is the roster-floor insurance. Bernard will stick on an NFL roster because he contributes on special teams — kick returns, coverage units, and gadget plays. That floor is important because it means he'll have a role even if the receiving production is modest.

The lack of a defining trait caps his ceiling. Bernard doesn't have elite speed, size, or suddenness, which means he can't create his own separation against NFL defensive backs. His production depends on schemed openings and favorable matchups, which limits his fantasy upside.
bernard.comp:
Kendrick Bourne — a reliable depth WR who produces modestly without elite traits. Like Bourne, Bernard's value is in his versatility and reliability rather than his playmaking ability.

The Bourne comparison is about the depth-receiver profile. Bourne has produced in the NFL because he's reliable and versatile — he can play multiple positions and contribute on special teams. Bernard has a similar profile, but he lacks Bourne's route-running polish, which limits his receiving upside even further.
bernard.dynastyOutlook:
WR6/7. Fringe roster player with return-league value. He'll stick on an NFL roster because of his versatility, but he won't produce enough to be a consistent fantasy starter.

The return-league premium is the only thing that makes him rosterable. In standard scoring, Bernard is a fringe player who'll give you 40-50 catches for 500 yards. In return-yard leagues, he adds kick-return contributions that elevate him to a usable WR5/6.

The Alabama system inflation is a concern. The Crimson Tide's offense creates schemed openings that inflate receiver production. Bernard's numbers may decline when he's not surrounded by elite talent and creative scheming.
bernard.badgersFit:
Bernard is a depth piece with return value. The ascending catches are nice but the lack of elite traits caps his ceiling. At pick 4.47 he's a reach — better WRs are available in this range. Only draft in return-yard leagues.
